back_translation: When we had reached the shore safely, we then learned that the island was called Malta.
The native islanders showed us extraordinary kindness, for they kindled a fire and welcomed all of us, because rain was falling and the weather was cold.
When Paul gathered a bundle of firewood and laid it on the fire, a viper crawled out because of the heat and bit his hand.

When the native islanders saw the dangerous creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, "Surely this man must be a murderer; even though he was saved from the sea, the goddess of justice has not allowed him to live."
But he shook the dangerous creature off into the fire and suffered no harm at all.

They expected Paul to begin to swell up or suddenly fall down dead. But when they had waited a long time and saw that nothing unusual happened to him, they changed their minds and began saying that he was one of the gods.
In the area near that place, there was an estate belonging to a man who was the chief official of the island, named Publius, who welcomed us and entertained us hospitably for three days.
Now the father of Publius lay sick with fever and dysentery. So Paul went in to visit him, prayed, laid his hands on him, and healed him.
When this had happened, the rest of the people on the island who were sick with various diseases also came to Paul and were healed.
They honored us with great honors and gifts, and when we were preparing to set sail, they supplied the provisions according to what we needed.
After three months had passed, we boarded a ship that had wintered at the island, a ship from the city of Alexandria, which had a figure of the Twin Brothers (Dioscuri) as a mark on its prow.
We put in at the harbor of the city of Syracuse and stayed there three days.
From there, we weighed anchor and continued sailing until we reached the city of Rhegium. The next day, a south wind came up, and on the second day we arrived at the city of Puteoli.
There we found brothers who believed; they begged us to stay with them for seven days. And so, we came to Rome.

The brothers who believed from Rome, when they heard the news about us, came out to welcome us, traveling far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Lodging-places. When Paul saw them, he gave thanks to God and was encouraged.
When we entered Rome, Paul was permitted to stay by himself, with one soldier guarding him.

After three days had passed, Paul called together the leaders of the Jews in Rome. When they had assembled, he said to them, "Brothers, I have done nothing against our people or against the customs of the ancestors, yet I was taken as a prisoner at Jerusalem and handed over into the hands of the Romans.
When they had examined me, they wanted to release me, because there was no reason for which I deserved to receive the death penalty.
But when the Jews objected, I therefore was compelled to appeal to Caesar — not because I had any accusation to bring against my nation.
Therefore, for this reason, I have invited you to meet and converse with me, because I am bound with this chain on account of the hope of Israel."
They replied to Paul, "We have not received letters about you from the province of Judea, and no brother from there has come to report or speak any evil concerning you.
But we consider it appropriate to hear what you think, because regarding this sect, we know well that people everywhere oppose and speak against it."

When they had set a day with Paul, a larger number of people came to him at his lodging. He explained and bore witness about the kingdom of God to them, and persuaded them to believe in Jesus, citing from the Law of Moses and from the words of the prophets, from morning until evening.
Some believed in what he said, but others did not believe.
They could not agree among themselves, so they began to depart, after Paul had spoken his last word: "The Holy Spirit spoke rightly through Isaiah the prophet, who said to your ancestors,
saying, 'Go, tell this people: You will hear and hear again, but you will not understand; and you will look and look again, but you will not see;

for the heart of this people has grown calloused, and their ears hear with difficulty, and they have closed their eyes, lest they should see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn back, and I would heal them.'
Therefore, let it be known to you that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen as well."
Paul stayed there for two full years in the house that he himself rented, and welcomed everyone who came to visit him.
He proclaimed the kingdom of God and taught about Jesus Christ the Lord with all boldness, without anyone hindering him.
